learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate knowledge of the diversity of Indigenous societies in Australia by reference to examples
Identify historical and social issues in relation to Indigenous and non-Indigenous relations in Australia
Demonstrate an awareness of ethical issues relating to research in Indigenous Australia
Identify anthropological approaches in relation to the representation of Indigenous peoples in ethnographic work
Discuss and write critically about particular case studies, especially using the required readings
Skilfully engage in academic research in the library and with electronic resources

assessments.due_date:
13/08/2026 - 22/10/2026

Your written submission is due at the conclusion of the seminar in weeks 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, and 12.
assessments.task_description:
A critical precis is a concise written piece that summaries and critically evaluates a text/s. You will be asked to write a critical precis that summarises, synthesises, and critically evaluates the week's topic (inclusive of required readings). During the seminar in identified weeks (see below), you will be asked to write your critical precis and will be required to submit it, on paper, by the end of the seminar.

You will aim to submit seven precis across the semester, in weeks 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, and 12. Each submission is to be 200 words in length (+/- 10%). You highest scoring five submissions will contribute towards your overall mark. Each submission will be graded out of six marks, with a total score out of 30 marks (5 submissions x 6 marks = 30 marks).

Highest marks will be awarded to those that raise original and insightful observations and/or questions.

Guidance on writing a critical precis will be provided in weeks 1 and/or 2.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must submit your assignment on paper by the due time, on the due dates.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Anthropologists often need to present theoretical and research material to a variety of audiences. To build your comprehension and oral presentation skills, you will be asked to lead a discussion about one of the topics covered during the semester in weeks 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11, and 12. Your discussion will occur in the week relevant to the topic.

Your discussion should include a brief presentation summarising the week’s topic, including contributions from required readings, and should pose a relevant and well considered discussion question generated from engagement with the material. Those who take a counterintuitive approach, raise original insights, and/or link the discussion to current events or personal experience will receive higher marks.

In addition to your discussion/presentation, you are required to submit a written reflection on the topic. You may like to consider discussing what you've learnt, what about the topic has changed your perception, or how it relates to your own personal experience. Your written reflection should be approximately 750 words (+/- 10% excluding references) and you will submit this at the end of the class after your presentation.

Topics will be allocated in weeks 1 and/or 2 of the course. Your options are:

* Early anthropology: Country and religion (week 4)

* 20th century anthropology: Country, resources, and ownership (week 5)

* 21st Century anthropology: indigeneity and belonging (week 6) 

* Water Country: relating to and managing freshwater and saltwater ecologies (week 7)

* Indigenous activism and the global Indigenous Peoples movement (week 9)

* Indigenous activism and the global Indigenous Peoples movement (week 10)

* Applied anthropology: practice, ethics and politics (week 11)

* Economic development, environmentalism and extraction (week 12)

You should aim for a presentation of approximately 10 minutes in length and may choose to use PowerPoint slides. You may co-present with a fellow student (or two), in which case you should each talk for approximately 10 minutes for a total of 20 minutes (for two speakers) or 30 minutes (for three speakers). You should present at least one relevant and well considered discussion question generated from engagement with the material. You should also be prepared to answer questions about the topic of your presentation.

If you choose to use PowerPoint, your presentation should include (at a minimum) an introductory slide with the topic and your name, and should conclude with a slide that lists your references.


Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The written component of this assessment item must be submitted at the end of class on the day of your presentation. No emailed assessments will be accepted.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Alongside conducting their own research, anthropologists are often required to review the research of others and critically examine their central arguments and methodologies in the context of current issues and debates in anthropology. This assessment requires you to write a critical review your chosen ethnography, and use two or more other anthropology references (such as articles, books, or book chapters), to help you write about key questions or arguments raised in the ethnography. In addition to the below, your review must describe the ethnography clearly, identify an argument, and be succinctly and clearly expressed.

At the start of semester, you will have chosen an ethnography to read from the list provided (below). 

Your review must:

1.Justify your choice of ethnography:

  • Why did you choose this one?
  • What interests you about the content/location/society?

2.Describe the ethnography’s content:

  • Who is the anthropologist writing about?
  • Where is their research located?
  • When did they undertake the research?
  • What methodologies did the anthropologist use to gather data?

3.Describe the book’s central argument: 

  • What has the anthropologist found out about the world?
  • What have they considered most interesting about the society or culture they are studying?
  • How do they explain or illustrate their key points?

4.Link to what you have learnt in the course

  • How does this ethnography speak to other topics we have covered?
  • How do other written works help to make sense of the content of the ethnography?

5.Raise at least one point of critique in regard to the central argument, using other references to back up your opinion. 


Note that ‘critique’ does not mean that you necessarily have to disagree with the author. You may be in agreement with the author, but you must justify this in a way which clearly highlights the strengths or weaknesses of the ethnography. We do not wish to hear unsubstantiated judgements of how ‘good’ or ‘bad’ the book is, but rather a considered critique of the author's contribution to the issues they describe.

Your review must be referenced in APA7 style. It must be presented in Times New Roman size 12 font, and double line spaced. It must include your name, student number, and a title at the top of the first page. 

It should be no longer or shorter than 2000 words (+/- 10% excluding reference list). 


List of recommended ethnographies (choose one):

Austin-Broos, D. (2009). Arrernte Present, Arrernte Past: Invasion, Violence and Imagination in Indigenous Central Australia. The University of Chicago Press.

Babidge, S. (2010). Aboriginal family and the state: the conditions of history. Ashgate.

Cowlishaw, G. (2004). Blackfellas, whitefellas, and the hidden injuries of race. Blackwell.

Dalley, C. (2021). What now: everyday endurance and social intensity in an Australian Aboriginal community. Berghahn.

Hinkson, M. (2021). See How We Roll: Enduring exile between desert and urban Australia.  Duke University Press.

Holcombe, S. E. (2018). Remote freedoms : politics, personhood, and human rights in Aboriginal central Australia. Stanford University Press.

Rose, D.B. (2024). Dreaming Ecology: nomadics and Indigenous ecological knowledge, Victoria River, Northern Australia. ANU Press.


Note: You must choose your ethnography by week 5 of the course and notify the course coordinator during class and begin reading. Please note you will also be expected to discuss your ethnography with the course coordinator during class time in week 8 to assist in preparing your review. Should you be unable to discuss during class time in week 8, please contact the course coordinator as soon as possible to arrange another discussion time.

Marking criteria: Criteria including rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Critical Anthropology

In this introductory seminar, we explore the question of belonging in contemporary Australia by looking at Welcome to Country and Acknowledgement of Country rituals. We set the tone for the course by beginning to examine critically the role of anthropology in producing knowledge about and with Indigenous Australians and the intercultural relationships that define Australia. We will also introduce the course content, objectives, assessment, and expectations of students.

To prepare for this semina you are expected to read the set readings for this week.
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Critical Anthropology In this introductory seminar, we explore the question of belonging in contemporary Australia by looking at Welcome to Country and Acknowledgement of Country rituals. We set the tone for the course by beginning to examine critically the role of anthropology in producing knowledge about and with Indigenous Australians and the intercultural relationships that define Australia. We will also introduce the course content, objectives, assessment, and expectations of students. To prepare for this semina you are expected to read the set readings for this week.

learning_activities.topic:
Cultural Diversity Across Indigenous Australia

In this seminar, we will examine the cultural diversity among Indigenous Australians and consider differences in how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people have experienced colonisation and intercultural relations.

During the seminar, you can expect to reflect on and discuss the week’s readings. We will also overview the list of ethnographies for the final essay. In addition to required weekly readings, you will read one ethnography throughout the semester and write a review of it for your major essay. See Assessment Tasks. NOTE: We will allocate topics for the presentation and written submission assessment during this week's seminar. If you cannot attend, you MUST make contact with the course coordinator who will assign you to a relevant week.
learning_activities.topic_description: Cultural Diversity Across Indigenous Australia In this seminar, we will examine the cultural diversity among Indigenous Australians and consider differences in how Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander people have experienced colonisation and intercultural relations. During the seminar, you can expect to reflect on and discuss the week’s readings. We will also overview the list of ethnographies for the final essay. In addition to required weekly readings, you will read one ethnography throughout the semester and write a review of it for your major essay. See Assessment Tasks. NOTE: We will allocate topics for the presentation and written submission assessment during this week's seminar. If you cannot attend, you MUST make contact with the course coordinator who will assign you to a relevant week.

learning_activities.topic:
Anthropological Literacy: Kinship and Relatedness

The study of kinship has traditionally been a cornerstone of anthropological enquiry, and Indigenous Australian kinship systems have long captivated anthropologists for their complexity and for what they reveal about social organisation and the interconnection of people, identity, land, morality, and religion. In this seminar, we introduce key concepts in the study of kinship and relatedness, using different case studies from across Australia.

During the seminar, we will reflect on and discuss the week’s readings including what anthropologists mean by kinship systems and social organisation. Why are these systems important to consider when seeking an understanding of Indigenous Australian societies?
learning_activities.topic_description: Anthropological Literacy: Kinship and Relatedness The study of kinship has traditionally been a cornerstone of anthropological enquiry, and Indigenous Australian kinship systems have long captivated anthropologists for their complexity and for what they reveal about social organisation and the interconnection of people, identity, land, morality, and religion. In this seminar, we introduce key concepts in the study of kinship and relatedness, using different case studies from across Australia. During the seminar, we will reflect on and discuss the week’s readings including what anthropologists mean by kinship systems and social organisation. Why are these systems important to consider when seeking an understanding of Indigenous Australian societies?

learning_activities.topic:
Early Anthropology: Country and Religion

One way that Anthropologists interested in the worlds of Indigenous Australians have tried to understand and analyse relationships to land is through the religious basis for land ownership. In this seminar, we will explore some of the fundamental concepts in studies of Aboriginal religion and cosmology, such as The Dreaming and examine its gendered aspects.

During the seminar, you can expect to work together to reflect on and discuss the week’s readings.
learning_activities.topic_description: Early Anthropology: Country and Religion One way that Anthropologists interested in the worlds of Indigenous Australians have tried to understand and analyse relationships to land is through the religious basis for land ownership. During the seminar, you can expect to work together to reflect on and discuss the week’s readings.

learning_activities.topic:
20th Century Anthropology: Country, Resources, and Ownership

In conjunction with their studies of religion and land ownership, anthropologists have also sought to understand how Indigenous peoples’ relationships to land, water, and other 'resources' are economic in character.

During this seminar, we will visit the Fryer Library to review the Caroline Tennant Kelly archive. You should read the required reading by Tennant Kelly and come to the seminar with at least two questions or observations that respond to the work or to the value of 20th century anthropological materials.
learning_activities.topic_title: 20th Century Anthropology: Country, Resources, and Ownership
learning_activities.topic_description: 20th Century Anthropology: Country, Resources, and Ownership In conjunction with their studies of religion and land ownership, anthropologists have also sought to understand how Indigenous peoples’ relationships to land, water, and other 'resources' are economic in character. During this seminar, we will visit the Fryer Library to review the Caroline Tennant Kelly archive. You should read the required reading by Tennant Kelly and come to the seminar with at least two questions or observations that respond to the work or to the value of 20th century anthropological materials.

learning_activities.topic:
21st Century Anthropology: Indigeneity and Belonging

Is Australia post-colonial and if so, what might that mean? Can anthropology be decolonised? This seminar will consider such questions from the perspective of Indigenous scholars and anthropologists engaged in thinking (and arguing!) about the politics of relations in Australia and other colonial nations.

Indigenous identity, belonging and political recognition are prominent in multicultural Australia and other settler-colonial nations. We will discuss how such claims unsettle settler-colonial society and how the Welcome to Country fits in this unsettling scene.
learning_activities.topic_description: 21st Century Anthropology: Indigeneity and Belonging Is Australia post-colonial and if so, what might that mean? Can anthropology be decolonised? This seminar will consider such questions from the perspective of Indigenous scholars and anthropologists engaged in thinking (and arguing!) about the politics of relations in Australia and other colonial nations. Indigenous identity, belonging and political recognition are prominent in multicultural Australia and other settler-colonial nations. We will discuss how such claims unsettle settler-colonial society and how the Welcome to Country fits in this unsettling scene.

learning_activities.topic:
Water Country: Relating to and Managing Freshwater and Saltwater Ecologies

The relationship between colonialism and the expropriation of Indigenous lands formed much of the basis of Australian anthropological knowledge. However, colonialism has also affected Indigenous relationships with water. In this seminar, we will explore how Indigenous Australians articulate cultural connections to water, how these connections have been fractured by colonialism, and how Indigenous Australians are reclaiming rights to water and associated territories. We will discuss approaches to protecting Water Country from different parts of the continent, exploring what this means for custodianship, governance, and law and policy. 

During seminar discussions, you will be encouraged to consider questions such as how do Indigenous people connect with water territories? What are some of the challenges for Indigenous-led water governance? How have some Indigenous communities responded to these challenges?
learning_activities.topic_title: Water Country: Relating to and Managing Freshwater and Saltwater Ecologies

learning_activities.topic:
Ethnography: Reading, Writing, and Review

This seminar will prepare students to read and review their ethnography for the final essay. It will discuss key concepts in ethnographic writing about Indigenous Australians relative to the early, 20th and 21st centuries. 

Each student should come prepared with a precis (summary) of the ethnography that you are reading (approx. 150 words) and at least three dot points regarding other anthropological themes, questions or points of critique. You may also wish to bring a draft outline of your essay.
learning_activities.topic_description: Ethnography: Reading, Writing, and Review This seminar will prepare students to read and review their ethnography for the final essay. You may also wish to bring a draft outline of your essay.

learning_activities.topic:
Indigenous Activism and the Global Indigenous Peoples Movement

This seminar will draw on discussions about indigeneity in anthropological literature to examine Indigenous peoples’ claims to distinctiveness, to a particular set of human rights, and to social justice in national and international contexts.
learning_activities.topic_title: Indigenous Activism and the Global Indigenous Peoples Movement
learning_activities.topic_description: Indigenous Activism and the Global Indigenous Peoples Movement This seminar will draw on discussions about indigeneity in anthropological literature to examine Indigenous peoples’ claims to distinctiveness, to a particular set of human rights, and to social justice in national and international contexts.

learning_activities.topic:
Identity, Art, and Cultural Representations

During this seminar, we will visit the UQ Anthropology Museum’s Veiled Histories exhibition. This visit will provide a practical and engaging introduction to how the creation of art produces aspects of identity.

Questions to consider may include: what role does museum curation play in the presentation of Indigenous artwork to a broad audience? How may the exhibition of Indigenous artworks challenge colonial narratives of power and knowledge production?

This visit will provide a practical and engaging introduction to how the creation of art produces aspects of identity. Questions to consider may include: what role does museum curation play in the presentation of Indigenous artwork to a broad audience? How may the exhibition of Indigenous artworks challenge colonial narratives of power and knowledge production?

learning_activities.topic:
Applied Anthropology: Practice, Ethics and Politics

Many anthropologists working in Australia do so outside of academia. This seminar will explore applied anthropology and its ethics and practice. We will focus on Native Title and land rights legislation in Australia. During the seminar we will discuss how anthropologists have been involved in applied contexts in Australia and what they do.

You should read the Australian Anthropological Society's Code of ethics and the AIATSIS Code of Ethics. Think about the context of anthropological involvement in applied work and using the Code, identify three issues that may be ethically or politically complicated for the work of applied anthropologists. In-class discussion will revolve around how such complicated situations may be resolved or responded to.

During the seminar we will discuss how anthropologists have been involved in applied contexts in Australia and what they do. You should read the Australian Anthropological Society's Code of ethics and the AIATSIS Code of Ethics. Think about the context of anthropological involvement in applied work and using the Code, identify three issues that may be ethically or politically complicated for the work of applied anthropologists. In-class discussion will revolve around how such complicated situations may be resolved or responded to.

learning_activities.topic:
Economic Development, Environmentalism and Extraction

In 2012, Prof Marcia Langton gave the Boyer lecture series titled The Quiet Revolution: Indigenous People and the Resources Boom. The views she put forward in these lectures sparked widespread critical discussion. In this seminar, we will examine the issues by focussing on questions of environmentalism (Green), Indigenous responses to mining (Black) and the broader questions of economic development. We will examine the importance of economic development, environmentalism and the social effects of mining for Aboriginal people in Australia.

Our discussions will focus on Indigenous peoples’ engagements with mining and resource extraction in remote Australia, and debates about cultural heritage management, Indigenous land claims, and environmentalism.

In this seminar, we will examine the issues by focussing on questions of environmentalism (Green), Indigenous responses to mining (Black) and the broader questions of economic development. We will examine the importance of economic development, environmentalism and the social effects of mining for Aboriginal people in Australia. Our discussions will focus on Indigenous peoples’ engagements with mining and resource extraction in remote Australia, and debates about cultural heritage management, Indigenous land claims, and environmentalism.

learning_activities.topic:
The Future of Critical Anthropology in Australia

Reflecting on the content of the course, this seminar will consider what a critical anthropology of Indigenous Australia might look like in 2026 and beyond. What are some of the issues that remain within anthropology as a disciple in reckoning with its past?

We will also take time to discuss any issues relating to your final assessment, provide an opportunity to refine ideas, and ponder where a career in anthropology may take you.
